Now, listen to this. Machine translation has been around from the 1980s. It's not AI. What AI does is what we know as post-editing. According to Wikipedia, "Post-editing (or postediting) is the process whereby humans amend machine-generated translation to achieve an acceptable final product."

There were attempts to use machine translation and low rate post-editing to replace actual translation in the past already. Not nice, but not sustainable either. So they made the AI do post-editing and pay even lower rates to humans to "evaluate AI material". It's essentially post-post-editing.

can correctly translate a "genderless pronoun" language into a "gendered pronoun" like English. The factor missing from digital translations? Deep understanding of context and culture. For us, if we cannot guess a subject's gender, we translate it in English as in our own native language: plural.

Then the EXTRAS land. Per market research report. Per voiceover artist. Per dubbing session. Per subtitle format. Per platform-specific cut. The "simple translation project" becomes a six-figure line item before a single new customer sees your brand.

That's the math we run now. One cost. Infinite markets. Real signal before real spend. If your translator's invoice is growing faster than your international revenue, you're not expanding. You're sponsoring someone else's expansion.

The pattern is everywhere. A US founder knows Germany wants the product. Japan wants the product. Brazil wants the product. But hiring local teams, translating assets, dubbing video, and tracking performance across regions destroys the runway.

I am not building a translation tool. I am building the infrastructure that makes geographic borders IRRELEVANT to revenue. Every business deserves access to global demand. Not just the ones with eight-figure expansion budgets.

The real friction sits upstream. You do not know WHICH markets want you. You do not know WHICH platforms convert there. You do not know WHICH voice, face, or format breaks through the local algorithm. Translation cannot answer any of that.

Global expansion stopped being a headcount problem. It became a leverage problem. The companies that understand this will own the next decade of cross-border revenue. The rest will keep paying agencies to translate decks nobody reads.
